## Long Rest Steps

A long rest is **declared during the Card Selection step** (instead of playing two cards). It counts as the character's **entire turn for the round** and is performed on **initiative 99**.

When a character long rests, they follow these steps **in order**:

1. **Lose one card of their choice** from their discard pile, then return all remaining cards in the discard pile to their hand. *(Mandatory)*

2. **Perform *Heal* 2, Self.** *(Optional)*

3. **Recover all spent items.** Items can even be used during the **same turn** in which they are recovered. *(Optional)*

---

A few key reminders:
- A long rest can only be performed if the character has **at least two cards** in their discard pile (so there is still a card to return to hand after losing one).
- Unlike a short rest, **you choose which card to lose** — there is no random element.
- Because the long rest happens on initiative 99, it will almost always occur **last** in the round.

*(Source: Frosthaven Rulebook)*

Here is the full rule for *Brittle* from the Frosthaven Rulebook:

> **Brittle:** The next time the figure would suffer damage from any source, they instead suffer double that amount of damage, and *Brittle* is then removed. *Brittle* is also removed if the figure is healed.

*(Rulebook, Negative Conditions)*

---

**Key points to remember:**

- *Brittle* is a **negative condition** that can affect both characters and monsters.
- It causes the **next instance of damage** to be **doubled** — then the condition is removed.
- It is removed in **two ways**:
  1. After the next damage is applied (doubled).
  2. If the figure is **healed** at any point (even before taking damage).
- The doubling happens *before* other damage interactions like *Ward* (which halves damage). For example, if a figure has both *Brittle* and *Ward*, the damage is first doubled (*Brittle*), then halved (*Ward*), resulting in the original amount.

Per the Frosthaven Rulebook (negative conditions):

> *Brittle*: The next time the figure would suffer damage from any source, they instead suffer double that amount of damage, and brittle is then removed. Brittle is also removed if the figure is healed.

A couple of clarifications from the rulebook's damage section:
- *Brittle* is applied **before** damage negation. When a character would suffer damage, *ward* and *brittle* are resolved first to determine the final amount, and only then can the character negate that damage with an ability or by losing cards.
- Healing removes *brittle* even if the figure is at full HP (just like *wound* and *poison*).

So in short: *brittle* makes the next instance of damage hit for double, then it's gone — unless you cleanse it with a heal first.

Here is a full breakdown of how *Advantage* works with attack modifier cards in Frosthaven, straight from the Rulebook (chunk 95–96):

---

## Advantage — Core Rule

> With advantage, the attacker draws two modifiers and uses one of them. A monster always uses the better one, but **a character may use either one.**

With *Disadvantage*, the attacker draws two modifiers and **always uses the worse one.**

---

## Rolling Modifiers with Advantage/Disadvantage

Things get more complex when *rolling modifiers* (cards with a rolling icon) show up:

1. **If the first draw is a rolling modifier**, keep drawing one at a time until you hit a non-rolling modifier. Then draw **one more** card, ignoring any rolling icon on it.
2. You now compare the **last two non-rolling modifiers**:
   - **With *Advantage*:** Use all the initial rolling modifiers **plus whichever of the last two you choose.**
   - **With *Disadvantage*:** Ignore all initial rolling modifiers and use whichever of the last two is **worse.**

3. **If the first draw is not a rolling modifier but the second draw is**, the rolling icon on the second card is **ignored** (it does not chain further draws).

---

## Ambiguity (Non-Numeric Effects)

> When there is ambiguity about which modifier is worse, the attacker must use the one drawn first.

This applies when comparing non-numeric effects like elemental infusions or negative conditions — these are treated as having a **positive but undefined value.**

---

## Multiple Advantage/Disadvantage

> An attack cannot gain multiple instances of advantage or disadvantage. If an attack has both advantage and disadvantage, it is considered to have neither.

---

## Automatic Disadvantage

> Any ranged attack on an adjacent enemy automatically gains *Disadvantage*.

---

### Quick Summary

| Situation | Result |
|---|---|
| *Advantage*, no rolling | Draw 2, pick the better (or either, for characters) |
| *Advantage*, first card rolling | Keep drawing until non-rolling, draw 1 more; use rolling chain + pick better of last 2 |
| *Disadvantage*, no rolling | Draw 2, use the worse |
| *Disadvantage*, first card(s) rolling | Keep drawing until non-rolling, draw 1 more; discard rolling chain, use worse of last 2 |
| Both *Advantage* and *Disadvantage* | Neither applies — draw normally |

*(Rulebook, p. 25 / chunk 95–96)*

Here's how *advantage* works with attack modifier cards in Frosthaven:

**Basic rule:** When an attack has *advantage*, the attacker draws **two** modifier cards and uses **one** of them.
- A **monster** always uses the better of the two.
- A **character** may choose either one.

With *disadvantage*, the attacker draws two and must use the worse one.

**Rolling modifiers complicate things.** If the first draw is a *rolling* modifier, you keep drawing one at a time until you get a non-rolling modifier, then draw **one more** modifier (its rolling icon, if any, is ignored). Then:
- With *advantage*: you apply all the initial rolling modifiers, and choose whichever of the last two (non-rolling) modifiers you prefer.
- With *disadvantage*: you ignore all the initial rolling modifiers, and use whichever of the last two is worse.

If the first draw is non-rolling but the second draw is rolling, the rolling icon on that second card is still ignored.

**Ambiguity:** When it's unclear which modifier is "worse" (e.g., comparing elemental infusions or negative conditions), the attacker must use the one drawn first. Any non-numeric effect counts as a positive but undefined value.

**Stacking:** An attack can't gain multiple instances of *advantage* or *disadvantage*. If an attack has both, they cancel out and it has neither.

Also worth noting: any ranged attack against an **adjacent** enemy automatically gains *disadvantage*.

Source: Frosthaven Rulebook, "Advantage and Disadvantage" (p. 26, rulebook chunks 95–96).

Based on the Frosthaven Rulebook, here's how **advantage** works with attack modifier cards:

## Basic Advantage Mechanic

When an attack gains **advantage**, the attacker draws **two modifiers instead of one** and uses one of them:
- **Monsters always use the better modifier**
- **Characters may use either modifier** (they have a choice)

## Advantage with Rolling Modifiers

If the first draw is a *rolling modifier* (a modifier card with a rolling icon), things work differently:

1. The attacker draws additional modifiers **one at a time** until a non-rolling modifier is drawn
2. They then draw **one more modifier** and ignore any rolling icon on it
3. The first non-rolling modifier and the one after it are compared
4. With advantage, the attacker uses **all the initial rolling modifiers plus whichever of the last two modifiers they choose**

Special case: If the first draw is not a rolling modifier but the second draw is, the rolling icon on the second modifier is still ignored.

## Ambiguity and Cancellation

- When comparing modifiers with ambiguous non-numeric effects (like elemental infusions or negative conditions), the attacker must use the modifier **drawn first**
- **An attack cannot have both advantage and disadvantage** — if it somehow gains both, it is considered to have neither

The Rulebook includes visual examples (Examples 1–4) showing how these rules apply in practice with different modifier combinations.

In Frosthaven, *advantage* changes how you draw *attack modifier cards* for **that attack target**:

Key details:

- Draw separately for each target of an attack. Each target gets its own modifier draw.  
- With *advantage*, draw 2 modifiers:
  - If the attacker is a **character**, they may choose either card.
  - If the attacker is a **monster**, it uses the better card.
- If the **first** card drawn is a *rolling modifier*:
  1. Keep drawing until you draw a non-rolling modifier.
  2. Then draw one more modifier.
  3. Compare the first non-rolling modifier and that final modifier.
  4. With *advantage*, use all the initial rolling modifiers plus whichever of the last two modifiers you choose.
- If the **second** card is rolling but the first was not, ignore the rolling icon on the second card.
- Multiple sources of *advantage* do not stack.
- If an attack has both *advantage* and *disadvantage*, it has neither.

So in practice:

  - If the **first card is non-rolling**, draw one more card and compare those two.
  - If the **first card is rolling**, keep drawing until you get the **first non-rolling** card, then draw **one more** card. Compare the **last two non-rolling results**; the rolling icon on that extra card is ignored.

Outcome:
- With *advantage*: use all the initial rolling modifiers, plus **whichever of the last two modifiers you choose**.
- With *disadvantage*: **ignore all initial rolling modifiers**, and use **whichever of the last two is worse**.

Tie/ambiguity rule:
- If it’s unclear which card is worse because of non-numeric effects, **use the one drawn first**.

In *Frosthaven*, if an attack gains *advantage*, you **draw two attack modifier cards** for that attack and **use one of them** (the attacker chooses; a monster uses the better one). If *disadvantage* is involved too, the attack effectively has **neither** advantage nor disadvantage.

### How the two modifier cards are used
- With *advantage*: draw **two**, then **choose one to apply** to the attack.
- With *disadvantage*: draw **two**, then you **must use the worse** one.

### Rolling modifier special case
If the **first** draw (with advantage/disadvantage) is a *rolling* modifier:
1. Keep drawing **additional modifiers one at a time** until a **non-rolling** modifier is drawn.
2. Then draw **one more modifier**.
3. Compare **the first non-rolling modifier and the one after it**:
   - *Advantage*: use the one you choose (but compare per the rule’s “rolling icon” handling).
   - *Disadvantage*: use whichever of those two is worse.

If the first draw is **not** rolling but the **second** draw is rolling, the rolling icon on the **second** modifier is **ignored**.

### When advantage happens (timing note)
Your attack modifier cards are drawn when resolving an **attack ability**, with one card drawn **per individual target** of that attack ability.

**Source:** Rulebook, *Advantage and Disadvantage* and *Attack Modifier Cards* sections.

Here's a full breakdown of **Looting** based on the Frosthaven Rulebook:

---

## The *Loot* Ability

> "*Loot X* is an ability that allows a figure to loot all loot tokens and treasure tiles within range X, including any in their current hex. This ability is unaffected by the presence of figures or overlay tiles. If there are no loot tokens or treasure tiles within the specified range, the ability cannot be performed."

*(Rulebook, p. ~37)*

---

## Looting Loot Tokens

When a **loot token** is looted, it is removed from the map. Then:

- **If a monster loots it:** Nothing else happens.
- **If a character loots it:** They draw one card from the **loot deck** (unless it's empty).
  - If the card provides a **random item**, the character draws from the random item deck and can use it for the rest of the scenario (even above item limits). If the random item deck is empty, it counts as a money card with 3 coins.
  - If the card provides **money or resources**, the character does **not** gain them until the **end of the scenario**.

---

## Looting Treasure Tiles

When a **treasure tile** is looted, it is removed from the map. **Monsters cannot loot treasure tiles.**

- If it is a **numbered treasure**, look it up in the Treasure Index and apply the effect.
- Only the looting character gains the reward — **unless** it is a random item blueprint or random scenario unlock (those are shared).
- If a character gains an item they cannot keep, they must **give it to another character or sell it immediately**.

---

## Key Reminders

- **Monsters and summons do not perform end-of-turn looting.** *(Rulebook, Appendix C)*
- Money and resources from loot cards are only collected **at the end of the scenario**, not immediately.

# Looting

Looting is how figures pick up loot tokens and treasure tiles from the map.

**The *Loot X* ability** (Rulebook, p. ~62):

> "*Loot X* is an ability that allows a figure to loot all loot tokens and treasure tiles within range X, including any in their current hex."

Key rules:

- *Loot X* is **unaffected by the presence of figures or overlay tiles**. If there are no loot tokens or treasure tiles within range, the ability cannot be performed.
- When a **loot token** is looted, it's removed from the map.
  - If a **monster** loots it, nothing else happens.
  - If a **character** loots it, they draw one card from the loot deck (unless it's empty).
    - If the loot card provides a **random item**, the character checks off a random-item slot in the scenario's loot table, draws from the random item deck, and adds it to their pool — usable for the rest of the scenario, even above the normal item limit. (If the random item deck is empty, the card is treated as a money card with 3 coins.)
    - If the loot card provides **money or resources**, the character doesn't actually gain them until the **end of the scenario**.
- When a **treasure tile** is looted, it's removed from the map. Monsters cannot loot treasure tiles. If it's a numbered treasure, look up the number in the Treasure Index and apply the effect.
- Only the looting character gains the reward, unless it's a random item blueprint or random scenario (those are shared with the party).
- If a character gains an item they aren't allowed to have, they must give it to another character or sell it immediately.

**End-of-turn looting** (Rulebook, Appendix C):
At the end of their turn, a character automatically loots their own hex (a free *Loot 0*, essentially). **Monsters and summons do not perform end-of-turn looting.**

Sources: Frosthaven Rulebook, "Loot" section (chunks 110–111) and Appendix C (chunk 221).

## *Loot* Ability

"*Loot X*" is an ability that allows a figure to loot all loot tokens and treasure tiles within range X, including any in their current hex. This ability is unaffected by the presence of figures or overlay tiles. If there are no loot tokens or treasure tiles within the specified range, the ability cannot be performed.

## What Happens When You Loot

**For monsters:** When a loot token is looted by a monster, it is simply removed from the map and nothing else happens.

**For characters:** 
- **Loot tokens:** When a character loots a loot token, it is removed from the map and they draw one card from the loot deck (unless empty). If the card provides a random item, they gain it immediately. If it provides money or resources, they don't gain them until the end of the scenario.

- **Treasure tiles:** When a character loots a numbered treasure tile, it is removed from the map. They then reference the treasure's number in the Treasure Index and apply the effect. Only the looting character gains the reward, unless it is a random item blueprint or random scenario. If a character gains an item they cannot gain for any reason, they must give it to another character or sell it immediately.

**Important note:** Monsters cannot loot treasure tiles.

Here's what the Frosthaven Rulebook says about the *Wound* condition:

> **Wound:** The figure suffers 1 damage at the start of each of their turns. *Wound* is removed when the figure is healed.

*(Rulebook, p. 29 / chunk 99)*

### Key points:
- *Wound* is a **negative condition**.
- It deals **1 damage at the start of each turn** of the affected figure — this applies to both heroes and monsters.
- It is **removed by any healing effect** (even a *Heal 1* is enough to remove it, though the healing and damage happen at different times).

### Interaction with *Regenerate*:
If a figure has both *Regenerate* and *Wound* at the start of their turn, the *Regenerate* effect (Heal 1, self) is applied **first**, which removes *Wound* before it can deal its damage. So the two conditions effectively cancel each other out that turn.

From the Frosthaven Rulebook (Negative Conditions, p. 29):

> *Wound*: The figure suffers 1 damage at the start of each of their turns. *Wound* is removed when the figure is healed.

A couple of useful interactions also called out in the rules:
- If a figure has both *Regenerate* and *Wound*, the *Regenerate* effect is applied first, which heals the figure and removes *Wound* before it can deal its damage that turn.
- Because healing removes *Wound*, even a *Heal 1* is enough to clear it.

Source: Frosthaven Rulebook, Negative Conditions section.

> Outside the cave, it is late. Much time has passed since you entered the cave, and that sinks a lead ball in your gut. You realize just how lost you really were and that, had Moonshard not arrived when she did, you would not be alive.
>
> Moonshard begins walking towards what you see is a circle of cloaked figures in the distance, all gathered around a small fire. You follow, and a voice calls out.
>
> "How did you find them?" A female voice asks, anxious for news.
>
> "Just like I said I would," Moonshard answers.
>
> "In danger then."
>
> "I believe my words were, 'about to get themselves flayed.'"
>
> You move close to the fire and Crain hurries to one side, eager to warm his hands. His face is gaunt, skin paled. You have no idea what the Quatryl saw down there in the dark, but you hope it was not too much.
>
> Looking over the fire, you can make out Moonshard's companion—a smaller Orchid—and you see that both her and Moonshard are quite old. The smaller one sits on a traveling stool and inspects you with easy purple eyes. Behind her are half a dozen other figures, each roughly the size of a human, and each hidden beneath heavy, wool robes.
>
> "I am thankful for your service, as always, my friend," the new Orchid says to Moonshard. And then to you: "We have waited very long to meet you. My name is Terra."
>
> "Well, ah, thank you," he says. He rubs his swollen jaw, trying to hide his suspicion. "But how is it you knew we would be here? Even I didn't know where exactly we were going this morning."
>
> "Because," Terra explains, "I have seen this moment in my meditations for the last two hundred years." Crain's eyes widen.
>
> "We have been planning for a long time," Terra explains. "All in preparation for the days that are now upon us—for the emergence of the Harbinger."
>
> At the mention of this name, the cloaked figures all rotate their heads to the east. One of them adjusts its hood, a long, bone-white hand emerging briefly and then disappearing back into its sleeve.
>
> "Our friends here are part of our preparations," Terra says. She gestures to one of the figures and it comes forward. "They are the Shattersong."
>
> The figure lowers its hood and reveals itself to be a Savvas of a unique and rather incredible form. Unlike other Savvas, whose bodies take on a variety of ochre or brown or slate, this one is made entirely of quartz that glimmers with the reds and yellows rising from the campfire. It would be beautiful were it not for its black eyes that stare at you with cold disregard.
>
> "We have been helping them," Terra says and rises to her feet. Her movements are slow, pained. It must have cost her a great deal to come here. "We have been fortifying this line of Savvas for generations now, providing them with only the purest of energy and sustenance and, because of that, the Shattersong are a lineage uniquely tuned to the thoughts and intentions of the Harbinger. Of malice and spite."
>
> Terra shuffles around the fire, moving a little easier now. "But you." She raises her eyes, sharp beneath her arched brow. "You play a crucial role here as well. The Harbinger means to invade everything around us, to break through the seals that contain it." She nods to Crain. "But with your ingenuity," then to you, "And with your might and courage, we have a slim chance of stopping it.
>
> "It's the best we can manage. The force we are trying to stop is far greater than any that exists on this plane. It is something of a miracle the Harbinger was ever bound at all, but there it sleeps, deep within the earth. In its dreams, it corrupts and plots its escape. Through its machinations, the seals grow weak."
>
> Crain jumps up excitedly. "Yes, the seals! This is what I've been trying to tell them all along. Logren wanted to strengthen the seals, but we require a device made of star iron!"
>
> Moonshard groans loudly. "Star iron is a myth used to lure gullible adventurers to their deaths. Don't worry, we'll get you the materials you need."

> **Brittle:** The next time the figure would suffer damage from any source, they instead suffer double that amount of damage, and brittle is then removed. Brittle is also removed if the figure is healed.

**Source:** Frosthaven Rulebook, Negative Conditions section.

---

**Key points to remember:**

- *Brittle* doubles the **next instance** of damage from **any source** (attacks, hazardous terrain, self-inflicted damage, etc.).
- It is removed **immediately after** that damage is applied.
- It is also removed if the figure is **healed**, even if no damage has been taken yet.
- Like all conditions, a figure cannot have multiple instances of *Brittle* — but gaining it again while already *Brittle* simply resets the duration (though since *Brittle* has no duration, this has no practical effect beyond confirming it stays active).
- Note that *Brittle* interacts with *Ward* (the positive condition that halves damage) — both effects are applied when calculating damage taken.
